sound_type: descending whinny trill -- bouncing-ball rhythm that accelerates and drops in pitch; 400-600 Hz; 3-5 seconds; the most distinctive rhythm of any owl call; soft, mellow, neighborhood-scale
ecosystem: Open forest, woodland edges, suburban neighborhoods, riparian corridors, parks. Year-round resident. Cavity nester. The most suburban owl in the PNW. Readily uses nest boxes.
season: Year-round resident. Calling peaks February-April (breeding season). Strictly nocturnal. Non-migratory.

coordinates.description: Western Screech-Owls resident in suburban neighborhoods, parks, and open woodland throughout the Puget Sound lowlands.

taxonomy_note.description: First Western Screech-Owl entry. Eighth consecutive Strigidae. Fifth genus (Megascops). ONE away from tying Picidae nonet record. Bouncing-ball trill. Bark mimicry camouflage. Most suburban owl. E=3 soft neighborhood voice.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Strigidae: ninth entry total. Eight consecutive at 135-142.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Megascops from Greek 'mega' (great) + 'scops' (owl). kennicottii honoring Robert Kennicott, 19th-century naturalist.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Megascops -- first entry. Fifth genus in the Strigidae run (after Bubo, Strix, Asio, Aegolius).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Strigiformes. Ninth entry. Eight consecutive at 135-142.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Strigidae continues for eighth consecutive degree. ONE away from tying Picidae nonet record. The owl era approaches the all-time mark.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Primary trill: 400-600 Hz. Lower than Saw-whet (~1000 Hz). Mellow, hollow quality.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Trill: 3-5 seconds. Repeated at 10-30 second intervals. Much shorter than Saw-whet's continuous minutes.
song_metrics.note_structure: BOUNCING-BALL TRILL: Notes accelerate and descend in pitch. Starts with widely spaced higher notes, ends with rapid low trill. The accelerating rhythm is diagnostic.
song_metrics.note_types: Bouncing-ball trill (primary, descending whinny), bark call (sharp, alarm), pair duet (mutual recognition).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single trill = one phrase. 3-5 seconds. Clear beginning (widely spaced) and end (rapid terminal trill). Repeated at intervals.
song_metrics.timbre: Mellow, hollow, whistled. Warm quality. Lower and softer than expected for an owl. The 'screech' name is misleading.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Low. Carries 100-200 meters in quiet conditions. Easily masked by suburban ambient noise. Neighborhood-scale.
song_metrics.tempo: Within trill: accelerating from ~3 notes/sec to ~8 notes/sec. Between trills: 10-30 second pauses.
song_metrics.context: Territorial advertisement, courtship, pair bonding. Males call from near nest cavities. Strictly nocturnal calling after full darkness.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Western PNW populations are gray-brown morphs. Interior populations may be grayer. Vocal variation minimal.
song_metrics.key_property: BOUNCING-BALL TRILL -- E=3 RHYTHMIC DESCENT. 400-600 Hz, 3-5 sec, accelerating and descending. Bark mimicry camouflage. Most suburban owl. Fifth genus (Megascops). Eighth consecutive Strigidae. ONE away from Picidae nonet tie.

plumage.overall: Gray-brown (western PNW) with intricate dark streaking and crossbarring mimicking tree bark. Short ear tufts. Yellow eyes. Small size (19-25 cm).
plumage.diagnostic_mark: Bark-mimicry plumage. Short ear tufts raised when alarmed (branch-stub posture). Yellow eyes. Small size.
plumage.flight_pattern: Direct, rapid wingbeats. Low flight. Nocturnal. Rarely seen in flight.

physical_description.overall: Length: 19-25 cm. Weight: 100-200 g. Wingspan: 46-61 cm. Small. Smaller than a pigeon. Short ear tufts.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: Small owl with ear tufts and bark-mimicry plumage in suburban/open woodland habitat. Bouncing-ball trill at night.
physical_description.field_id: Listen for the bouncing-ball trill at night in suburban neighborhoods, parks, and open woodland. The rhythmic acceleration is unmistakable once learned. Day roosts in tree cavities or pressed against trunks -- nearly invisible.

nodes.title: Bouncing-Ball Trill -- Rhythmic Descent
nodes.description: 400-600 Hz, 3-5 seconds. Notes accelerate and descend in pitch like a ball bouncing to rest. The rhythm is the diagnostic feature. E=3 soft, mellow, neighborhood-scale.

nodes.title: Bark Mimicry Camouflage
nodes.description: Plumage mimics tree bark with intricate streaking and crossbarring. Ear tufts + narrowed body = broken branch stub. Invisible against trunk. Pressed against a tree, the owl disappears.

nodes.title: The Most Suburban Owl
nodes.description: Commonly nests in backyard trees and nest boxes. Permanent resident. Generalist diet (insects, mice, crayfish). Invisible neighbor. Many homeowners unaware they have a Screech-Owl.

nodes.title: Eighth Consecutive Strigidae (135-142)
nodes.description: Eight consecutive owl entries. Five genera. Six species. ONE away from tying the Picidae nonet record of nine. Two consecutive E=3 entries (141-142). The quiet zone deepens.

nodes.title: The E=3 Quiet Zone (141-142)
nodes.description: Two consecutive E=3 entries -- the quietest stretch in the Strigidae run. Saw-whet (141, monotonous) and Screech-Owl (142, rhythmic). Energy descent: E=6 peak at 138, now E=3 at 141-142.

cross_references.relationship: E=3 PAIR! Two consecutive E=3 entries. Both quiet, nocturnal, small. Saw-whet: deep forest, monotonous tooting. Screech-Owl: suburban, bouncing-ball trill. Different rhythms at the same energy.
STRIGIDAE EIGHT! Eight consecutive. Five genera. Six species. ONE away from Picidae nonet record. The owl era approaches the all-time mark.
PREDATOR-PREY! Great Horned Owls prey on Screech-Owls. A Screech-Owl hearing a GHO hoot freezes in terror. Predator and prey documented in the same Strigidae run.
